region_of_origin: East Asia (China, Japan, Korea); widely cultivated across Southeast Asia

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226723002 covers radish broadly; no specific daikon/Chinese radish code identified. Closest parent match used.

serving.description: 1 medium serving (approx. 100 g sliced or grated)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw Chinese radish is approximately 100 g, equivalent to several thick slices or a small bowl of grated daikon.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Radish (Daikon), Raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chinese radish (daikon), raw. Input record cr_id F14040044 carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95. Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entry #169247 (Radishes, oriental, raw) which reports energy 18 kcal, protein 0.6 g, fat 0.1 g, carbohydrate 4.1 g per 100 g — fully consistent with HPB values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for daikon/white radish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test for Chinese radish (daikon) raw is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated from the closest analogous entry for raw white radish / daikon in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (15 × 4.1) / 100 = 0.62, rounded to 0.6. This is consistent with the very low carbohydrate content and high water content of raw daikon.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (21 mg/100 g) with high potassium (227 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable K:Na ratio of approximately 10.8:1
hypertension.guidance: Raw Chinese radish is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals due to its very low sodium content and high potassium, which supports vascular tone regulation; however, pickled daikon preparations must be avoided as they contain substantially elevated sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.1 g/100 g), low GI (~15), low glycemic load (0.6 per 100 g serving), and dietary fibre 1.6 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw Chinese radish has a very low glycemic index and glycemic load, making it a safe and beneficial vegetable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; it can be consumed freely as part of a low-GI dietary pattern, while sweetened or pickled preparations should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, low caloric density (18 kcal/100 g), and dietary fibre supporting lipid management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw Chinese radish is cardioprotective by virtue of its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium-to-sodium ratio, and dietary fibre content, and can be incorporated freely into a heart-healthy diet; avoid high-sodium pickled preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C (~22 mg/100 g) and sulforaphane-related anti-inflammatory compounds from the Brassicaceae family
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw Chinese radish provides antioxidant vitamin C and anti-inflammatory glucosinolate-derived compounds that may support management of rheumatoid arthritis; raw consumption is preferred over prolonged cooking to preserve these bioactive compounds, and pickled preparations should be avoided due to high sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese radish (daikon), consumed raw, is a large white elongated root vegetable belonging to the Brassicaceae family (Raphanus sativus var. longipinnatus). It is widely used across East and Southeast Asian cuisines — particularly Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and Singaporean cooking — where it appears raw in salads, pickles, garnishes, and as a crunchy accompaniment. Raw daikon has a firm, crisp texture and a mild, slightly peppery flavour that becomes sweeter toward the tip. Nutritionally, raw Chinese radish is very low in calories (18 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates (4.1 g/100 g), with a very low glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 0.6 per 100 g serving, making it an excellent vegetable for blood glucose management. It provides 0.6 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 2.5 g sugar per 100 g. Its sodium content is very low at 21 mg/100 g while potassium is relatively high at 227 mg/100 g, yielding a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio that supports blood pressure management. Calcium (27 mg), iron (0.4 mg), and vitamin C (~22 mg) are also present. The vegetable is naturally cholesterol-free and contains negligible saturated fat. Clinically, raw daikon is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is commonly known as lobak putih (Malay) or 白萝卜 (Chinese) and is used in dishes such as chai tow kway (carrot cake), soups, and stir-fries.

pickled side dish (avoid for sodium-restricted diets)

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040044, confidence 0.95) and independently cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central #169247 (Radishes, oriental, raw), which reports identical macronutrient values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for raw white radish/daikon, clearly documented. Ontology codes are defensible and cross-checked.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA FoodData Central #169247 which reports 0.017 g/100 g, rounded to 0.0). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (naturally zero for a raw vegetable). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food contains no cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food contains no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — raw root vegetable contains no meaningful vitamin D). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA FoodData Central reports trace amounts below 0.05 g/100 g but HPB is the primary source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item; USDA FoodData Central provides some of these values but HPB is the designated primary source for this Singapore-context record. vitamin_c_mg of 22.0 mg/100 g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central #169247 as a supplementary value not present in the HPB entry. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for raw daikon/white radish; no direct GI test for this exact preparation is available. Keto dietary tag retained from input as carbohydrate is 4.1 g/100 g, consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds.
